summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the parties were engaged in a long marriage followed by a significant period of separation – Consideration of contributions – Where the parties agree as to the specie of property to be divided – Property adjusted 65 per cent to the wife and 35 per cent to the husband – No matters of principle.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

Mr Adar (“the husband”) commenced proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) by way of an Initiating Application filed on 15 November 2024 seeking orders for the adjustment of property pursuant to s 79 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). Ms Adar (“the wife”) filed a Response to an Initiating Application on 24 March 2025 seeking different orders as to the adjustment of the property of the parties.
The proceedings were transferred to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1) on 4 August 2025.
The parties married in City D in 1965. There are two adult children of the marriage, born in 1969 and 1970. They are currently 57 and 55 years of age.
The wife contends the parties separated in July 2003. The husband contends the parties separated on 1 January 2009. It is agreed that a finding as to the date of separation is not material for this determination. An Order for Divorce was made in early 2024.
The parties agree as to:
(a) The construction of a balance sheet identifying their property, liabilities and their values (Exhibit 3) at $36,887,164; and

(b) The form of orders adjusting their property in specie except as to $6,350,000 held in a controlled monies account being part of the wife’s funds produced from the recent completion of the sale of her property at E Street, Suburb F NSW (“the [Suburb F] property”) (item 2 of Exhibit 3).

The husband contends it is just and equitable to divide the property of he and the wife as to 60 per cent to the wife and 40 per cent to himself. On that basis, he would receive the monies in the controlled monies account. The wife contends it would be just and equitable to divide that property as to 80 per cent to herself and 20 per cent to the husband. On that basis, she would retain the monies in the controlled monies account.
For the reasons that follow, the wife will receive 65 per cent and the husband will receive 35 per cent of their property. Consequently, the funds in the controlled monies account are divided 70.9 per cent to the husband and 29.1 per cent to the wife.

BACKGROUND

The husband was born in 1941. He is currently 84 years old and in good health.
The wife was born in 1943. She is currently 82 years old and in good health.
At the time of the marriage the parties lived in an apartment owned by the wife’s father at Town G, City D. They did not pay rent. The wife worked as an educator. The husband also worked as educator at H College, City D.
From February 1968 to February 1970 the parties lived in City J, living in rental accommodation. The wife worked in a service role prior to the birth of their first child in 1969. The husband worked as an educator at K College.
The parties returned to Sydney in early 1970 and lived in a unit owned by the wife’s father at L Street, Suburb M NSW (“the [Suburb M] property”). They did not pay rent. The husband commenced trading in his own business. He obtained funds to establish the business and to purchase a motor vehicle from the wife’s father. The funds were advanced by way of loan and the loan was repaid. He also worked part-time as an educator at N University.
In 1970, the parties’ second child was born.
The Suburb M property was sold in late 1974. The wife’s father provided $15,000 from the proceeds of sale to each of the husband and the wife.
In April 1971, the wife’s father established O Pty Ltd. The wife and her father were directors of that corporation. The wife began to receive dividends and continued to receive dividends and other payments from that corporation and other entities in which her father had an interest throughout the marriage until his death in 2000. There is no direct evidence particularising the value of these dividends and payments.
In March 1972, the Suburb F property, then owned by P Pty Ltd, was transferred to O Pty Ltd for $85,000. A home was constructed on the property. The construction completed in 1974. The wife’s father, or an entity controlled by him, paid for the construction costs. The husband gave evidence, that I accept, as to he and the wife attending architect meetings and otherwise being engaged in the construction of the home.
The parties and the children moved into the Suburb F property in November 1974. The husband gave unchallenged evidence as to he purchasing furniture for use in the home.
In 1975 the wife commenced study.
In January 1977, the Suburb F property was transferred from O Pty Ltd to the wife. The wife paid $275,000, agreed to be sourced from her family and/or its entities, with the balance of the acquisition being funded by a mortgage of $282,947 from Q Pty Ltd, an entity controlled by her father for estate planning purposes. The terms of the advance from Q Pty Ltd did not specify a date for repayment of principal or attract interest.
In 1978, the husband leased his business and took a sabbatical year as an educator at R University. The parties and the children moved to State S. The wife received rental income from the Suburb F property.
In 1980 the parties and the children returned to Sydney and recommenced living at the Suburb F property. The husband was appointed to a senior position at T School.
From 1989 to 1992, the husband was appointed to a senior position at U University. The husband purchased a property at V Street, Suburb W, Queensland (“the [City X] property”) for $215,000 funded by O Pty Ltd (possibly by way of a mortgage) and the wife. The wife travelled between City X and Suburb F.
In 1993 the husband was appointed to a senior position at Y University.
In late 1996, O Pty Ltd purchased 2 B Street, Town C NSW (“the [2 B Street] property”) for $470,000.
In March 1997, the wife was diagnosed with cancer and underwent three surgeries in April 1997. In 1997 the husband was appointed to a senior position at N University. The parties were again living at the Suburb F property.
In late 1997, the City X property sold for $205,000. From the proceeds of sale $142,000 was deposited into the husband’s Commonwealth Bank of Australia account (Exhibit 5). The husband gave evidence in his affidavit that these funds were applied to meet the family’s living expenses. This evidence was not directly challenged by the wife. The husband was unable in his oral evidence to particularise the application of these funds almost 30 years ago. The wife did not suggest that he wasted the funds or that he did not use them for the benefit of the family. I accept that he did apply them for the benefit of the family.
On 28 July 1999, the wife discharged the Q Pty Ltd mortgage on the Suburb F property with a tranche of funds she received from the winding up of her father’s companies.
The wife’s father died in 2000. The wife received $2,600,000, being one third of the proceeds of sale of her father’s property at Z Street, Suburb F, and in excess of $3,000,000 by the conclusion of the winding up her father’s company, AA Pty Ltd.
In mid-2000 O Pty Ltd transferred the 2 B Street property to the parties as tenants in common in equal shares for $600,000. The purchase was wholly funded by a mortgage of $600,000 from O Pty Ltd. That mortgage was discharged by the wife from her inheritance. The husband entered an interest free mortgage over his share of the property in favour of the wife for $312,438.
In early 2001, the wife purchased two off-the-plan developments in her sole name at BB Street, Suburb CC NSW (“the [BB Street] property”) for $725,000 and DD Street, Suburb CC NSW (“the [DD Street] property”) for $880,000. In mid-2001, the wife sold the BB Street property for $901,000. In mid-2002, the wife sold the DD Street property for $985,000.
From late 2001 the husband commenced to increasingly spend time at the 2 B Street property. He says that he commenced to occupy it in 2002. The wife says this occurred in July 2003.
From 2003 the husband commenced to run livestock on the Town C properties. The wife remained living at the Suburb F property. The wife says that she continued to undertake tasks at the Town C properties until 2005. The husband says he cannot recall the wife so attending. The difference in their evidence is not material.
In November 2003, the parties purchased as tenants in common in equal shares the property at 1 B Street, Town C NSW (“the [1 B Street] property”) for $1,751,613. The purchase price was funded by the wife’s inheritance from her late father’s estate. It is the adjoining property to the 2 B Street property (the 2 B Street property and the 1 B Street property being collectively referred to as the “[Town C] properties”). The husband provided the wife with a mortgage of $878,000 over his share of the property.
In 2004 the husband commenced a business at Town EE, working part-time for three days per week. He sold the business in late 2023 for $350,000. He gives unchallenged evidence that I accept of applying the proceeds of sale towards living expenses, maintenance and improvements to the 2 B Street property. Extensive improvements have been occasioned to the Town C properties over the past 20 years, funded in part by each party.
In 2006 O Pty Ltd was deregistered and the wife paid tax in the range of $725,000 to $750,000.
In 2007 the wife transferred about $1,000,000 from her inheritance to her superannuation.
In December 2023, the husband established FF Pty Ltd.
In September 2024, the husband retired from his profession.
In August 2025, contracts for the sale of the Suburb F property were exchanged for $28,400,000. The settlement of the unencumbered property completed in February 2026. The wife received $21,573,850 from the proceeds of sale, with a further $6,350,000 from the proceeds being deposited into a controlled monies account with her solicitors.
Each of the parties have provided funds and support for their children and grandchildren, including paying school fees absent criticism from the other.

THE RELIEF SOUGHT BY THE WIFE

Exhibit 1, the orders sought by the wife, and Exhibit 2, the orders sought by the husband, are identical as to paragraphs 1 to 3 inclusive and 5 to 9 inclusive. Each record:
That within eight weeks of the date of these Orders the wife shall do all acts and things and sign all documents necessary to:-
1.1 Transfer to the husband the property at [1 B Street], [Town C] in the State of New South Wales, being all of the land contained in Folio Identifier [...] (“[1 B Street]”).

1.2 Transfer to the husband the property at [2 B Street], [Town C] in the State of New South Wales, being all of the land in Folio Identifier [...] ([2 B Street]”).

1.3 Simultaneously with the transfers herein the wife shall hand over a Discharge of Mortgage with respect to the mortgage in which the wife is mortgagee and the husband is mortgagor secured on each of the said [Town C] properties respectively.
That pending the transfer of the [Town C] properties to the husband in accordance with Order 1 herein neither party shall encumber, transfer or deal with their respective interest in the [Town C] properties except in compliance with these Orders;
The husband shall pay all outgoings and expenses associated with the [Town C] properties including but not limited to rates, electricity, water, insurance, maintenance and repairs as and when they fall due and all arrears outstanding as at the date of these Orders.
...
Except as otherwise provided for herein, each party shall retain to the exclusion of the other all other assets and resources in their respective sole name or possession, including but not limited to:-
5.1 all jewellery and other chattels currently in their custody possession or control;

5.2 any motor vehicles registered in their name;

5.3 any superannuation entitlement in their name;

5.4 any funds standing to each of their credit in any Bank, Credit Union or Building Society account.
That for the purpose of transfer of real property referred to in these Orders:-

6.1 each of the parties will instruct their representatives to do all acts and things necessary to give effect to the settlement via an electronic conveyancing platform, such platform to be agreed between the parties and in default of agreement within seven days of the date of these Order to be PEXA ("the electronic conveyancing platform").

6.2 in the event either party is self-represented at any time, then that party will nevertheless instruct a Conveyancing Practitioner, including a registration or other Agent qualified to do land and registry services registration, who subscribes for to the electronic conveyancing platform for the purposes of the settlement and will meet their own costs of the Practitioner instructed by them;

6.3 the parties will pay their own fees for participating on the electronic conveyancing platform, and in the event one party meets the other party's fees at the date of settlement, the party who did not pay their own fees will reimburse the other party within seven days of the settlement.
Except as otherwise specified in these Orders, each party be solely liable for and indemnify the other against any liability currently in their respective sole name.
The parties shall do all acts and things necessary and give all consents and execute all documents and writings to give effect to these Orders in the time periods prescribed.
That in the event that either party refuses or neglects to execute any deed, document or instrument necessary to give effect to these Orders, the Registrar of the Court be appointed pursuant to Section 106A of the Family Law Act to execute such deed, document or instrument in the name of the said party and do all acts and things necessary to give validity and operation to the deed, document or instrument upon the Registrar being provided with verification of such refusal or failure by way of affidavit.
By way of Exhibit 2, the husband seeks:
That within 7 days of the date of these orders, ATW Family Law shall pay to the husband the sum of $6,243350,000 currently held in a controlled monies account in the wife’s name including all interest earned.
(Emphasis added)
By way of Exhibit 1, the wife seeks:
That within 7 days of the date of these orders, ATW Family Law shall pay to the wife, or as she may direct, the sum of $6,350,000 currently held in a controlled monies account in the wife’s name including all interest earned on.

THE LAW

In determining claims for alteration of property interests between married couples, I am required to:
(a) Make findings as to the identity and value of the existing legal and equitable rights and interests in any property of the parties or either of them (including superannuation interests), and their existing liabilities at the time of the trial;

(b) Take into account considerations as to contributions which are listed in s 79(4) of the Act so far as they are relevant;

(c) Take into account considerations relating to current and future circumstances which are listed in s 79(5) of the Act so far as they are relevant; and

(d) Ensure that any order made is just and equitable.

The property of the parties – s 79(3) of the Act

The identity and value of the property and liabilities of the parties were agreed (Exhibit 3) as follows:
ASSETS
Item	Ownership	Description	Applicant's Value	Respondent's Value
Property

LIABILITIES

	Member	Fund & Interest	Applicant's Value	Respondent's Value

	SUMMARY

	Total property		$35,105,214	$36,295,652

	Total liabilities		$0	-$1,190,438

	Total superannuation		$1,781,950	$1,781,950

	Total financial resources		$0	$0

	TOTAL	$36,887,164	$36,887,164
(As per the original)

Is it just and equitable to adjust property?

In Stanford & Stanford [2012] HCA 52; (2012) 247 CLR 108, the High Court observed that it is necessary for me to be satisfied that justice and equity will be achieved as part of the adjustment process pursuant to s 79 of the Act. The requirements identified in the High Court are readily satisfied in this matter having regard to:
(a) The long period of the relationship of the parties and the myriad of contributions made over that period;

(b) The parties’ relationship having broken down and them now living apart;

(c) The concession of each party as to property being adjusted to one another and the inability of the parties to continue to jointly own the Town C properties; and

(d) Title to property needing to be changed or adjusted when consideration is given to the s 79(4) and s 79(5) factors identified below.

The parties agreed that:
(a) A single pool approach as to superannuation and non-superannuation property is appropriate;

(b) The conduct of either party during the marriage and up to the time of trial did not make the contributions of the other more onerous than they ought to have been;

(c) Any paid legal fees would not be the subject of consideration by way of s 79(4) or s 79(5) of the Act; and

(d) Any embedded capital gains liability in the Town C properties (not the subject of evidence) would not be a relevant factor considered by way of s 79(5).

CONTRIBUTIONS

At the time of the marriage the husband contributed a motor vehicle and a bank balance of a few thousand dollars. The wife contributed fixed interest deposits of about £6,835, shares, and a motor vehicle.
The parties generally kept separate financial identities throughout the marriage. I accept the husband’s unchallenged evidence that there was collaboration between he and the wife as to the financial decisions made for the family.
The wife facilitated the family’s international relocation on three occasions and other relocations within Australia to enable the husband to generate income for the family and to pursue his career endeavours. The wife submits that her role in the marriage “sacrificed her own career aspirations”. Throughout the marriage the husband worked in academia. I accept his evidence that he applied all his income for the benefit of the family, including meeting some household expenses and the cost of travel and holidays. He purchased furniture and other items for the Suburb F property.
The wife contributed income from her employment from time-to-time for the benefit of the family. She additionally contributed from 1971 dividends and payments from her father’s corporations and entities that were applied for the family and household expenses permitting the wife to accumulate funds to acquire properties. The husband conceded that the income by way of dividends the wife received was of “great assistance” in meeting the day-to-day expenses of the family but did not know the value of those dividends and payments.
The wife’s evidence was that she paid “the majority” of the household bills and expenses. The wife conceded in cross-examination that she was unaware of her financial circumstances during much of the marriage, they being managed by her father and others. She conceded that the husband also paid household bills. It is difficult to weigh this evidence absent particularity. The wife met the cost of maintaining the Suburb F property, including having it regularly painted, repaired and cleaned. I find the husband contributed to the costs of the household commensurate to his income.
The evidence does not support a finding as sought by the wife that the husband’s financial and parenting contributions were diverted from 1980 to 1993 when he was engaged in an extramarital affair.
The husband acknowledges that the wife’s father contributed on behalf of the wife by:
(a) Providing accommodation for the parties while living in City D from 1965 to 1968;

(b) Providing accommodation for the parties while living in Suburb M on their return to Sydney from 1970 and providing $15,000 to each of the husband and the wife in 1974 when the Suburb M property was sold; and

(c) The Suburb F property, being acquired by way of the wife paying $275,000 sourced from her family and/or its entities and through a favourable mortgage advance from Q Pty Ltd ultimately discharged by monies received by the wife sourced on the winding up of her father’s company.

The income received by the wife enabled her to contribute $140,000 to the purchase of the City X property.
The wife contributed her inheritance after the death of her father of $2,600,000, being one third of the proceeds of sale of her father’s property at Suburb F. She contributed more than $3,000,000 from winding up her father’s company AA Pty Ltd.
These funds enabled the acquisition of the Town C properties and were applied to the wife’s superannuation.
Each of the parties contributed to the costs of improvements to and the maintenance of the Town C properties. I find that the husband paid a greater proportion of these costs than the wife. The husband contributed his efforts and labours over 20 years to the improvement and conservation of these properties. The wife contributed through maintaining the Suburb F property for the past 25 years.
The wife made contributions by adopting within the marriage dynamic the role of primary homemaker and parent of the children. The husband, as part of that dynamic, subject to his employment commitments, made contributions supplementing the wife’s homemaking and parenting roles.
I find that each of the parties contributed to the best of their abilities within their marriage dynamic that commenced in 1965 and evolved over many years thereafter. This dynamic continued in a not dissimilar fashion after each party commenced to occupy separate residences.
The wife submits that there would be “no real property” to divide between the parties “without [her] family”. She identifies that her family provided funds from the sale of the Suburb M property enabling her to own the Suburb F property, purchase the City X property, and acquire the Town C properties, in addition to her dividend income.
It is her case that, in addition to receiving significant income from her family during the relationship, “[she] introduced all the major items to the balance sheet”. Although not expressed in these terms, the import of her submission was that these contributions ought to be recognised and attract substantial weight when regard is had to the usage of those contributions in the accumulation of the current property of the parties. The wife contends that contributions ought to be found to be as to 80 per cent in her favour and 20 per cent to the husband.
The husband submits that the wife’s quasi-mathematical approach, attempting to quarantine assets in the assessment of contributions, particularity the product of the Suburb F property sale, is not appropriate in the circumstances of each of the parties’ making contributions in different ways over the many years of their long marriage.
The husband’s Case Outline (Exhibit 7) records:
It is common ground that the [Suburb F] Property significantly increased in value during the marriage and post-separation due to market forces. The parties are at odds as to how the Court should treat that increase in value
The husband submits that the wife attempts to undervalue and isolate his contributions made over a very lengthy period. That said, he accepts and recognises the significance of the contributions made on behalf of the wife through the generosity of her father, and the contributions from her inheritance. He submitted that contributions ought to be assessed as to 60 per cent in favour of the wife and 40 per cent to him.
Neither party submitted that there must be a relationship between the contributions and what they produced. Longstanding High Court and Full Court authority reinforces a holistic approach being taken to the assessment of the myriad of contributions in this matter over the period of 61 years, being from the time of the marriage, after separation and up until the hearing, as prescribed by s 79(4) of the Act (Mallet v Mallet [1984] HCA 21; (1984) 156 CLR 605; Shaw and Shaw [1989] FamCA 29; (1989) FLC 92-030; Singerson v Joans [2014] FamCAFC 238 at [66]; Jabour & Jabour [2019] FamCAFC 78; (2019) FLC 93-898, citing with approval Dickons v Dickons [2012] FamCAFC 154; (2012) 50 Fam LR 244). Both parties endorsed that holistic approach being taken.
In Dovgan & Dovgan [2021] FamCA 306 Harper J restated (at [347]), apposite to the circumstances in this case, the need to holistically assess contributions, and that “all contributions must be weighed collectively and so it is an error to segment or compartmentalise the various contributions and weigh one against the remainder”.
Taking into account all these matters, I conclude that contributions ought to be assessed favouring the wife as to 65 per cent and the husband as to 35 per cent. In dollar terms, this equates to $23,976,657 to the wife and $12,910,507 to the husband, a difference of $11,066,150.

SECTION 79(5) CONSIDERATIONS

The husband sought an adjustment to the contribution finding at whatever value was necessary such that he would achieve 40 per cent of the property of the parties grounded from a consideration of the factors identified in s 79(5)(b) and specifically the respective adjusted property of each party consequent on the contribution finding. The value of that adjustment is 5 per cent, or in dollar terms, an additional $1,844,358 being a differential of $3,688,716.
The wife opposed any adjustment to the contribution finding on consideration of s 79(5) factors.
Each of the parties are self-funded retirees supporting themselves from their not insignificant property and resources. Neither has re partnered.
The husband by way of the agreed division of property in specie and the contribution finding will have the benefit of residing at the unencumbered Town C properties growing livestock, superannuation in excess of $1,000,000, his savings of more than $220,000 and some of the funds held in the controlled monies account from the sale of the Suburb F property.
I do not accept that the consideration of s 79(5) factors warrants any adjustment to the contribution finding.

CONCLUSION – JUSTICE AND EQUITY

Accordingly, the property of the parties will be adjusted 65 per cent to the wife and 35 per cent to the husband, a difference of $11,066,150 in favour of the wife
The husband’s overall entitlement to 35 per cent of the property of the parties identified in the balance sheet equates to $12,910,507.
The husband currently has in his possession or will receive the following:
Item	Description	Value
Assets
3	1 B Street, Town C NSW	$3,250,000
4	2 B Street, Town C NSW	$3,600,000
8	Commonwealth Bank account #46	$3,293
10	Commonwealth Bank account #24	$111,595
11	Commonwealth Bank account #32	$109,714
12	Commonwealth Bank account #74	$1,173
13	Commonwealth Bank account #74	$1,343
19	HH Limited Shares	$2,851
20	Livestock	$85,000
21	Farm equipment	$6,000
Total	$7,170,969
Superannuation
28	Super Fund 1	$683,532
29	Super Fund 2	$554,402
Total	$8,408,903
The husband accordingly will hold $8,408,903. He ought to hold $12,910,507. He therefore requires $4,501,604 from the proceeds of sale of the Suburb F property held in the controlled monies account at item 4 in the balance sheet, being 70.9 per cent of those funds. The parties agreed that such funds be paid to him within seven days of the date of the orders
The wife’s overall entitlement to 65 per cent of the property of the parties identified in the balance sheet equates to $23,976,657.
The wife currently has in her possession or will receive the following:
Item	Description	Value
Assets
1	Balance of net proceeds of sale retained by wife, excluding Item 2 of E Street, Suburb F NSW ($27,923,850.13	$21,573,850
6	Loan due mortgage 1 B Street, Town C NSW	$878,000
7	Loan due mortgage 2 B Street, Town C NSW	$312,438
14	Commonwealth Bank account #96	$429
15	Commonwealth Bank account #50	$9,801
16	GG Bank account #42	$165
Total	$22,774,683
Liabilities
22	Mortgage over 2 B Street, Town C	$312,438
23	Mortgage over 1 B Street, Town C	$878,000
Total	$1,190,438
Superannuation
30	Self-Managed Super Fund 1	$544,016
Total	$22,128,261
The wife holds $22,128,261. She ought to hold $23,976,657. She therefore requires $1,848,396 from the proceeds of sale of the property held in the controlled monies account at item 4 in the balance sheet, being 29.1 per cent of those funds. An order will be made for those funds to be paid within seven days of the date of the orders.
Ther wife in her Response to an Initiating Application sought the husband pay her costs of the proceedings. The husband’s attitude to costs is less clear, albeit he has capacity to make an application for costs should he elect to do so. Orders will be made to ensure each party will have the capacity to make such applications for costs as they are advised. If no application is made within 28 days, any extant applications for orders for the costs of the proceedings are dismissed.
Standing back, I find the distribution of the property of the parties in the terms identified above is appropriate and otherwise just and equitable. Orders will be made accordingly.
